Report
Rescue Below the Moons of Eden by Elusive Faith
0 Reviews Table of Contents Download ePub Chapter or Story
[This is an unauthorized, unofficial sequel to "Under the Moons of Eden, by Christopher Leeson]

Years after the war with the Asymmetrics has ended, the Earth Alliance has sent out survey crews to map out new territories ceded as part of the cease fire. The crew of the Peregrine stumble upon a planet they couldn't have anticipated.
Categories: Sci-Fi Gender Change
Characters: Female to Male, Adult (27-62 yrs)
Motivation for Change: Forced
Sexual Orientation: Changes to be Attracted Only to Men
Story Theme: Distant Future, Fan Fiction, Romance
Type of Change: Fast/Instant Change, Stuck
Series: None
Chapters: 17 Completed: Completed Story
Word count: 62585 Read: 4776
Published: 04/12/26 Updated: 04/13/26
Author's Chapter Notes:
[This is an unauthorized, unofficial sequel to "Under the Moons of Eden, by Christopher Leeson]
##Chapter 2##

On occasion, I do apply myself on non-piloting related matters, so I offered up the flight ‘s mission planning room for our efforts to make contact with the surface. Barely the size of a college dorm room, it was a far sight less cushy as Bey’s briefing room, but it would suffice for our needs. I was already there getting our central planning table up and running in preparation to host my guests to arrive.

Two hours after Bey set us to task, the security captain stepped in cautiously, looking around the cramped room like he was having second thoughts, too polite–or too nervous to step inside.

I waved him in, gesturing to a stool next to the table, “Relax, Captain. Come in, roll up your sleeves. You’ll find we’re quite a bit more relaxed here than in the commander’s briefing room.”

He nodded and stepped inside, opting to forego the stool and remain standing. “Yes sir, thank you sir.”

The double sir.

Maybe it was the security forces in him, or maybe he was not quite used to senior officers that weren’t as uptight as Bey. I settled on a still, casually folding my arms across my chest, “What’s your name, Captain?”

Meyer blinked as if confused by what I assumed had been a very straightforward question. But he reacted as any good military man ought to–directly, “Captain Meyer, sir.” He hesitated for a moment, then added, “We attend Lieutenant Colonel Bey’s meeting everyday, sir.”

This guy was priceless–all the wide-eyed wonderment of Kuch minus the bad luck, “Yes, Captain, I am aware. I was asking what your first name was.” Maybe leading by example here would go a long way–I stuck out my hand, “For example, I’m Ian.”

“Right,” Meyer nodded, accepting my hand and giving a firm, single pump, “Richard, sir.”

Again, I offered him the stool, hoping now he would allow himself to relax, “So what do you think about all this stuff the scope geeks dug up?”

He remained standing, “Scope geeks, sir?”

“Take a look around, Rich. You see these four walls? When you’re in here, I’m Ian, not sir. I promise I’m not going to bite your head off for suspending rank for an hour or two.”

“Yes sir–er–Ian.”

Finally, a bit of progress, “Good man.”

“Sir–Ian–What’s a scope geek?”

“It’s the flight crew’s name for me and the rest of the mission crew.” Captain Harwell was leaning against the open bulkhead door, but for how long was anybody’s guess.

“A loving moniker, Harwell, I assure you.” I said, though maybe my sincerity was undercut by the slight smirk I failed to scrub off my face.

It was true that the other pilots and I called the mission crew officers ‘Scope Geeks’, but it wasn’t because of any sort of animosity—it was simply a little bit of inter-occupational rivalry. We were ‘Bus Drivers’ and they were ‘Scope Geeks’.

A man had to have a sense of humor about things out in the middle of the void.

Of course, it didn’t help matters that back in flight school there was a long-running trend that if an officer couldn’t hack it behind the stick, they’d shift career paths to be sensor operators.

Captain Harwell, though, never went through flight school as far as I could tell—he was born to be a sensor operator from the very start, so maybe lumping him in with the rest of the scope geeks was a tad presumptuous.

Or, maybe, it fit him even better.

Harwell smirked and stepped into the room, eyeing me the entire time but clearly speaking to Meyer, “All pilots think they’re at the top of the pecking order, but take away their engines and what they? Lawn dart operators.” He sat down on one of the stools and began to load his data onto the table display, “the whole crew is like an ecosystem, Major, every element supports the other and together we get the mission done.”

That was fair. I took a moment to swallow my ego and Meyer nodded thoughtfully, as if Harwell had just laid down some profound, ancient wisdom. Humbly clearing my throat, I offered a small peace offering, “Okay, fair point. No more scope geek nonsense.”

Now Harwell wore the smug grin, “Thank you, Major.”

“Unless? Maybe there is some room for negotiations?” I reached deep and pulled out the first alternative to come to mind, “Scope jocks?”

Harwell just smirked.

Maps of the surface, the same we’d seen during the brief, appeared on the digital surface of the table, highlighting everything determined to be artificially made—roads, farm plots, buildings. He’d clearly done a lot of preparation ahead of time. “Captain Leen and I developed an algorithm that picks out terrain features that do not naturally form. Everything unnatural gets highlighted and now we can see where the population centers are concentrated.”

“Looks like you’ve got everything pretty much covered, Harwell. Almost seems like you don’t need us here for any of this.”

Meyer leaned forward, taking the liberty of manipulating the maps with his hands, “Actually, Ian—” he glanced up at Harwell, then me, then slid back into old habits, “Actually, sir, there is still quite a bit of work we need to figure out here. We’ll need to find a discrete place to land—someplace far enough that we aren’t noticed but not so far that we can’t get back to the shuttle if things get hot.”

I nodded, “Good thinking. We can’t assume that just because these are humans that they’re friendly.”

The three of us began to pour over the scans, searching for the right mixture of elements to maximize our tactical entry and exit from the surface. Some locations offered great cover, but were either too far from a settlement or the settlement was too small to find much value in. Larger settlements seemed ideal; they were more developed, better infrastructure—better intel on the why and how they’d come to inhabit Vetters Beta.

It took a good four hours of planning, but after several bouts of healthy debate, the three of us came to agree upon a suitable landing point and a settlement to make contact with.

Harwell reached up high with both arms, stretching side to side before slipping off his stool, “So do you think the commander will approve the plan?”

Mission planning had worn me out, too. I Stifled a yawn behind a closed first and shrugged, “You’ve briefed for him many times. You know he’s going to poke and prod, nit pick every decision we’ve made. And, to be fair, this is our first planetary landing this whole deployment, so it makes sense he’s going to want to comb over every aspect.”

Meyer frowned, “So you think we should keep going?”

“Not at all,” I shook my head and glanced over our plan once more—it was solid, “Leave the brief to me, I’ll handle any curve balls the Lieutenant Colonel could throw at us.”

+

“We’ve selected this group of settlements here along the southern edge of these foothills,” the indicated foothills lit up on the digital overlay in the briefing room, illustrating our plan to Bey and the others. “Given the proximity of each of these settlements, we estimate that they are interconnected and part of a larger group. Our landing site will be in the hills themselves, they will offer us the option to select which settlement to visit after our initial planetside assessment.”

“What factors went into your selection of these particular settlements, Major?” Bey was on his feet, leaning over the table and tracing his finger along the dirt road network connecting the settlements.

“These larger settlements appear to serve as a central hub for the surrounding areas. The network of roads, while still essentially dirt roads, show a level of sophistication not seen in the peripheral sites. Whoever these people are, I am confident we will get most of our answers from them.”

Bey continued to stare at the screen, searching for gaps in our plan to quiz us on. The room sat quietly for five minutes before he stepped back and sat down, “I agree with your assessment, Major.” He turned his attention to Meyer, “What are the tactical considerations, Captain?”

Meyer stood and began without hesitation, “We’ll arrive by shuttle just before dawn, landing in the foothills as the Major briefed. It’s early and there is no electricity in these settlements, so the chances of someone being awake to spot us are low, preserving our initiative.”

“Two squads of ten led by Lieutenant Kessler and myself, plus Major Doughty who will pilot the shuttle and act as your liaison with the surface.”

Seemingly satisfied with our plan, Bey nodded, “Thank you, gentlemen. Begin preparations—mission launches in sixteen hours.”

+

“What gives, Ian?” Nick intercepted me in the chow line at the mess hall a few hours before the mission was scheduled to launch. “I got a look at the shuttle manifest—you’re not taking a co-pilot with you? What? Did you just want to see me beg or something? Because I’m not above that.”

Thanks to his sudden appearance out of nowhere, I’d missed my bowl and dolloped a scoop of oatmeal onto my tray, “How did you see the manifest?”

“Lieutenant Raleigh owed me a favor, forgave his debt. But don’t change the subject, Ian. I ask again—what gives?”

I started to scoop the oatmeal into my bowl with a spoon, shaking my head, “As much as I would love to see you pleading on your knees, Nick, it wasn’t my call. Bey didn’t like the idea of risking half the flight crew should things go tits up. Besides, if things do go south, he’s going to want a capable pilot to swoop down and rescue us.”

He blinked, smiled, and offered me a napkin, “I’m a capable pilot.”

“And that’s why you’re my recommendation for our contingency plan.”

Immediately, Nick’s demeanor perked up, “Really? Ha! Well damn, now I feel bad about spitting your eggs.”

Defiantly, I grabbed a fork full of egg and stuffed it into my mouth without breaking eye contact.

After that moment of silliness, we sat down at our usual table. “Just don’t go telling Trevor or Kuch that I did you any favors—especially since Trevor is supposed to be up next in the assignment rotation.”

“Even if you had given him the assignment, I would have just used that trump card I won off him the other day.” That was the last bit to be said on the matter before he switched up topics, “So how do you think these people even got here? Think they could be Assie sympathizers or something?”

“I haven’t the slightest idea, Nick. Right now, my biggest worry is that we’re going to get all the way down there and none of them are going to speak a lick of English.”

“If that happens, just fall back on the old tried and true universal greeting.”

I tilted my head, “Refresh my memory, which universal greeting is that?”

Upon asking the question, Nick sat up, leaning forward in his chair and, in a comically nasally voice, declared, “We come in peace. Take me to your leader.”

+

At 0600 sharp I raised the landing gear and pulled one of the two shuttled out of the Peregrine’s rear bay and set course for Vetters Beta’s surface. In the back, decked out in their state-of-the-art Eart Alliance body armor, were twenty highly trained soldiers ready to defend me should the inhabitants reveal themselves to be hostile.

Every ship in the Earth Alliance had their own contingent of security forces—which was just a fancy name for infantryman—assigned to the crew to assist in defending against any sort of boarding action by a hostile force. This basically meant that Captain Meyer and the rest of his team had pretty much been sitting around with nothing much to do besides disassembling and reassembling their rifles over and over.

And now they were going to get their first real taste of action in twelve months.

Halfway through the transit to the surface, Captain Meyer’s second, Lieutenant Kessler, poked his head into the cockpit, “Mind if I join you, sir?”

I motioned to the open seat, “Be my guest.”

“I’ll try not to accidentally bump anything here. Wouldn’t want to cut the engine by mistake or something.”

I smirked, “I appreciate that, Lieutenant. I know we’re all eager to get to the surface, but we pilots preferred controlled descents over dropping like a rock.”

He settled into the other seat, successfully avoiding knocking into anything sensitive, “It must take a lot of time to train you pilots. Lots of buttons and knobs—”

“—And switches,” I offered while flipping on the auxiliary thrusters.

Kessler stared through the forward window but rather than looking curious, he seemed a bit nervous. “What’s on your mind, Lieutenant?”

Looking like I’d just caught him with his hand in the cookie jar, Kessler let out a cautious huff while glancing back towards the rest of the security forces, “Sorry, sir, I was just feeling a bit nervous about things. Thought getting a view of the surface would help shake off the nerves.”

“You didn’t serve in the war, did you?”

He shook his head.

“This is your first mission, and you didn’t want to look scared in front of your men, did you?”

“Is it that obvious, Major?”

“And you thought I wasn’t going to give you shit about it, did you?”

He didn’t quite seem to get the gentle tease I was poking him with, maybe I failed to inject right amount of levity to my tone, but Kessler frowned, “Well, sir, I had to weigh the options of looking weak in front of nineteen men I see every day or a major I’ve only seen a couple of times from across the mess hall.”

I’d hit a nerve and felt like a jerk for it. Maybe I could steer this more constructively, “So now that you’re up here, why don’t you tell me your thoughts on Captain Meyer?”

“Sir?”

“He’s a good soldier, isn’t he? Has a smart head on his shoulders, right?”

Kessler nodded readily, “Of course, sir. Captain Meyer is a good leader and looks out for us.”

Nodding, keeping my eyes on the terrain ahead, “So you trust him—trust his judgment.”

“Absolutely, sir.”

“Then it occurs to me that, maybe, he chose you for this mission for a reason—don’t you think, Lieutenant?”

That got a nod, and a small smile, from him, “I get your meaning. Yes, sir. Thank you, sir.”

“Ian.”

“Sir?”

I offered over my hand, “It’s just us up here, you can call me Ian.”

Kessler nodded, accepting my hand, “Of course… Roland.”

Transit to the surface took only thirty minutes from gear up to touch down. I brought the shuttle in for landing at the designated spot just as the sun was starting to breach the horizon. I flipped a switch and the entry ramp deployed, settling down onto soft terrain which was quickly trampled when all twenty security forces piled out to secure the immediate vicinity.

Meyer have the all-clear and Kessler called back to me in the shuttle. I disembarked and drew in my first breath of an alien world. It was thick with dust and the scent of sap, bringing back childhood memories of time spent camping in the forest preserves. If I hadn’t just brought us down from orbit, I would swear that we were back on Earth.

It was just a knee-jerk feeling, though. As the sun continued to rise, it started to become readily apparent that this was not Earth. Some of the vegetation was green and there were tall plants that resembled trees and there were even colorful buds that looked like flowers. But everything was just different enough that, when taken as a whole, emphasized just how different this place was.

“Okay, on me everyone,” Meyer rallied the two teams, and they gathered into a large circle around him, myself, and Kessler. “Sergeant Pilmer’s squad is going to remain here at the shuttle with me. We’ll do a little light recon but remain in sight of the shuttle.”

“Sergeant Shaw’s squad is with Lieutenant Kessler. You’re to escort Major Doughty to the nearest settlement. Keep radio chatter to a minimum but report in every thirty minutes.”

In a single, unified motion, everyone clapped their hands like footballs players breaking out of a huddle. I pulled out a map of the terrain and oriented myself, “Alright Lieutenant, I hope your land navigation skills aren’t as rusty as mine. How do we get out to the road?”

Kessler, showing a good deal more confidence now than he had during our conversation, gently took the map for a quick study. He looked up and scanned the horizon once, then nodded his head toward one direction, “That way, sir.” Then, to his squad, “Wedge formation, Sergeant Shaw. Private Boucher, take point. Keep safeties on unless I say otherwise.”

It only took about a hundred meters before I felt myself transported back to my training days in officer candidate school on Tyche. Way before flight school Nick and I spent days out in the field, hiking through forest and up mountains, through swamps and across prairies with nothing but a ruck sack, compass, and a map. At the time I could confidently say that I was adequate, but that was nearly ten years ago at this point and my skills had waned more than a little bit.

Fortunately, Kessler was more than on top of things. If we hadn’t just spent the past year deployed, I’d believe it if he said he’d completed his land navigation training within the past month.

Following his lead we steadily made our way down out of the foothills and into a series of plowed fields along a gentle slope. While the sun was up, it was still early, and I couldn’t spot any workers tending to the growing crops.

Beyond the fields we finally came upon the sophisticated dirt road I’d been staring at for the past few days on the scans and now that I was up close and personal with it, I could tell some considerate throught had gone into its construction. It was three meters across with a crown profile to prevent water from building up and creating a muddy mess. Each side had scooped ditches for further drainage.

The ditches were currently dry. Kessler had us lay low within them to stay out of sight while we reassessed our surroundings. “Squad Bravo in position, waypoint three.”

Meyer’s voice chirped back over the radio a second later, “Copy Bravo.”

I climbed up the side of the ditch, staying low up rising high enough to see clearly in both directions of the road. Down to the right, about half a klick I could see several figures walking on the road. It didn't appear that they’d spotted us yet.

I slid back down into the ditch next to Kessler, “Okay, we’ve got three, maybe four people heading our way.”

“What do you want to do, Major?”

“Give me two of your men while you stay out of sight and follow along the ditch.”

Without wasting a moment, Kessler turned to his Sergeant, “Shaw, Argueta, accompany the Major along the road.”

Both soldiers popped up and swiftly scaled the ditch. Shaw offered me a hand and together we started down the road towards the approaching people.

+

It didn’t take long before the group four—two men and two women—spotted us along the road. At first it seemed as though they took us for locals, but that didn’t last long, and they stopped with a good deal of commotion about fifty meters away.

“Sergeant, sling your rifles. Let’s try to appear as friendly as possible, shall we?”

Shaw and Argueta did as I instructed, eliciting a positive response from the group as they began tentatively approaching us again.

We waited, giving me a chance to silently rehearse what exactly I was going to say to these people—it was the one thing I neglected to consider during the entire planning process despite all the care and time we’d put into the mission. In fact, the only words that were readily coming to mind was the stupid universal greeting Nick had joked about.

“W-we come in peace… take me to your leader.”

One of the women and both Shaw and Argueta burst into laughter thanks to my awkward delivery of such a ridiculous phrase. Silly as it was, it seemed to break the silent tension between both groups and one of the men stepped forward to address us.

“Forgive us for hesitating, but we do not see many new faces around here and we’ve certainly never seen anyone armed before.” He was a lean-looking man, toned like he’d spent plenty of time toiling the fields under the warmth of the Vetters sun. “May I ask where you’ve come from? How long have you been here?”

“This is Sergeant Shaw and Corporal Argueta, and I’m Major Doughty with the Earth Alliance. We’ve only just arrived and were curious about your settlement. I don’t suppose we could go somewhere to talk?”

On the surface, everything seemed quite congenial, the man motioned to himself and then indicated the others each in turn, “I’m Jason Madsen, this is Mara Lipkin and they are Liam and Evelyn Stokes.”

They all waved in turn, but it was Mara who spoke next, “The Assies, they didn’t take your weapons?”

I shook my head, but Argueta stepped in before I could explain the situation, “War ended three years ago.”

“As Corporal Argueta said—yes, the war with Asymmetrics ended three years ago. We’re part of a survey team mapping out several systems ceded to us as part of the armistice and we were quite surprised to discover that this planet was already inhabited.” Gesturing around, I asked, “If I may, how long have you all been here for? You said the Assies took your weapons, are you prisoners?”

At the news a mild level of excitement passed through the group before us. They took a few moments to exchange handshakes and firm hugs before Jason turned back to me to take my hand—which I offered reflexively. “Sir, we’ve been here since the Assies dropped us off six-some years ago. There are other groups and colonies here that were marooned here in a similar fashion.”

“So this is like a prison planet?”

Jason nodded, “Had little but the clothes on our backs before the Assies kicked us to the curb and forgot about us. We haven’t met anyone new since we arrived.”

“Does this mean we finally get to go home?” Liam asked.

The other didn’t seem to share the same excitement. In fact, they appeared worried, ambivalent at best. His wife—or I assumed it was his wife given their shared last name—
reached for his hand, grabbing it tightly, “This is our home now, Liam… right?”

“Evelyn, they’re here to rescue us—we can finally get back to civilization!”

The tension between the four began to rise, clearly some of them appreciated this simple existence far more than the others. Whether they were ready to escape or not, I had to break the news, “I’m afraid we are not equipped to rescue anyone right now. It is just pure happenstance that we discovered you were even here.”

“But now that we do know you are here, I am certain the Earth Alliance is going to be sending support this way as soon as possible.”

Liam seemed appeased but the others still looked like I had just kicked over their sandcastle.

“Maybe you should speak with our mayor,” Jason said after taking a moment to silently console Mara and Evelyn. “But I’m afraid we can only bring one of you into town.”

“Why is that?” Shaw asked suspiciously.

“It’s a long story, but I promise you will be completely safe.”

What was the likelihood of this being a trap? If my name were Kucharski, I’d say almost certainly, but these four had no idea that we’d be showing up so what were the chances they’d have something nefarious planned?

I could practically hear Nick’s voice calling for the odds.

I led Shaw and Argueta away several steps so we could speak quietly, “As Lieutenant Colonel Bey’s Liaison, I’ll go with them and speak to their mayor. Meet back with Lieutenant Kessler and let him know that if I am gone for more than three hours, consider this settlement hostile.”
Chapter End Notes:
[This is an unauthorized, unofficial sequel to "Under the Moons of Eden, by Christopher Leeson]
